s1_b1: Heat a glass of ice water and watch the thermometer.
s1_b2: You're pouring energy in steadily. But while ice remains, the temperature does not budge. Zero. Still zero.
s1_b3: Energy in, no temperature rise. The heat is going somewhere we can't see on the thermometer.
s1_b4: Zoom in. In ice, particles are locked in a rigid lattice. The incoming energy isn't speeding them up —
s1_b5: — it's breaking the bonds that hold them in place. Energy spent on freedom, not on speed.
s1_b6: Only once the last of the ice is freed does the thermometer start climbing again.
s1_b7: That hidden energy — paid to change state, not to change temperature — is the latent heat.
s1_b8: The same pause happens again at boiling. Energy floods in to tear liquid into vapor — temperature holds flat.
s1_b9: And it runs in reverse. Steam releases that hidden energy when it condenses—which is why steam delivers far more heat to skin than boiling water at the same temperature.
s1_b10: It's why sweat cools you: evaporating water carries off enormous hidden energy as it leaves your skin.
s1_b11: A full heating curve, then, has flats and slopes. Slopes warm it. Flats rebuild it.
s1_b12: Computing the heat absorbed during melting and boiling from the mass and the latent heat — that's yours.
